fingerprint: Ellipse optimization target IS the globe: distance_analysis.py grid-searches b/a and orientation against explicitly labeled 'great circle' distances — spherical geometry is the fitness function sculpting the ellipse

globe_values.quantity: Semi-major axis a = 20,015 km = π × R_Earth
globe_values.note: Same fingerprint as disc_radius (Section 4.5.9 / EXP-003). Applies to WIN-030 because a is the ellipse's semi-major axis.

globe_values.globe_derivation: Great-circle distance = R_Earth × arccos(sin φ₁ sin φ₂ + cos φ₁ cos φ₂ cos Δλ). By definition, these are distances measured along a spherical surface.
globe_values.match_precision: exact (by definition — the code labels them 'great circle')
globe_values.note: CRITICAL: distance_analysis.py's reference dict is explicitly commented 'Actual distances (km, great circle)'. The grid search optimizes ellipse parameters to minimize error against these spherical distances. The ellipse shape is literally sculpted by the globe.

globe_values.globe_derivation: This polynomial approximates the radial distortion factor of an azimuthal equidistant projection of a sphere. On a true flat disc, no latitude-dependent correction would be needed — distances would be Euclidean. The correction exists because the disc IS a projection of a sphere.
globe_values.match_precision: structural (polynomial fitted to absorb spherical distortion)
globe_values.note: formulas.json FORM-052 defines a quadratic lat correction (R²=0.787). This correction has no physical meaning on a flat disc — it's a polynomial approximation to the sec(latitude) distortion that arises from spherical-to-flat projection.

globe_values.globe_derivation: This is the equation of an ellipse with semi-major axis 20,015 km (= πR_Earth) and semi-minor axis 8,537 km. The firmament's cross-section is an elliptical curve whose horizontal extent is set by the globe's semi-circumference.
globe_values.match_precision: exact (uses 20015 = πR_Earth as the boundary radius)
globe_values.note: The alternative dome profile tested in test_curve_stretching.py is literally an ellipse equation with its radial boundary at πR_Earth. Even when the model tries different curve shapes, the globe's radius anchors the geometry.

files_checked: generate_advanced_map_v2.py — ellipse visualization: a=20015, b=15000, orient=60°. No computation, just hardcoded plotting.
find_curve.py — 8 city pairs with hardcoded WGS84 reference distances. No ellipse comparison.
test_curve_stretching.py — tests H(r)=8537·√(1−(r/20015)²) semicircle profile. Binary search solver for city coordinates.
build_complete_api.py — disc_shape='ellipse', ellipse_a=0.70, ellipse_b=0.93, major_axis_degrees=60. Status: 'approximate'.
inject_ai_layer.py — disc_radius_km=20015 only. NO ellipse parameters. Core model uses circular disc.
monitor.py — DISC_R=20015.0, R_EQ=14105.0. No ellipse monitoring. WIN-030 not tracked.
predictions.js — WIN-030 NOT FOUND in confirmed_wins array (file ends at WIN-026).
api/current/parameters.json — disc_radius=20015 only. No ellipse params. how_derived='V45 derivation from southern route distances'.
api/current/formulas.json — FORM-051/053: AE projection lat_to_dome_radius. FORM-052/054: quadratic correction ratio. No ellipse formula.
scripts/distance_analysis.py — THE KEY FILE: grid search over b/a=[0.70..0.90], orient=[45..75°], optimizing against 'great circle' distances.
code_search.found_in: distance_analysis.py (grid search), generate_advanced_map_v2.py (hardcoded a=20015, b=15000, orient=60°), build_complete_api.py (disc_shape metadata), test_curve_stretching.py (semicircle profile using 20015)
code_search.notes: No script derives b/a or orient from dome geometry. All ellipse parameters come from grid-searching against great-circle (spherical) distances. The dome's core model (inject_ai_layer.py) doesn't even use the ellipse — it uses a circular disc with radius 20015. The ellipse exists only in the map visualization and distance analysis scripts.

structural_analysis.what_dome_claims: Elliptical disc geometry (V5+) improves inter-city distance predictions over circular geometry, with b/a converging across versions.
structural_analysis.what_actually_happens: The distance_analysis.py script defines reference distances as 'great circle' distances (spherical geometry by definition), then grid-searches over b/a and orientation to minimize absolute error against those spherical distances. The ellipse's shape is determined by the sphere. This is not the ellipse 'discovering' the disc's physical shape — it's the ellipse absorbing the anisotropic distortion that an AE projection introduces when mapping a sphere to a plane.
structural_analysis.why_no_flat_disc_derivation: On a truly flat elliptical disc, the b/a ratio and orientation would be derivable from first principles (physical measurements of the disc's boundary). Instead, the parameters are fitted to great-circle distances computed on a sphere. No dome equation produces b/a ≈ 0.75 from disc physics. The ellipse is an empirical correction to a projection artifact.
structural_analysis.inconsistency_in_dome_code: inject_ai_layer.py (the dome's core parameter file) defines disc_radius_km=20015 with NO ellipse parameters. build_complete_api.py defines disc_shape='ellipse' but marks it 'approximate — V10 refinement in progress'. The dome model can't agree with itself on whether the disc is circular or elliptical.
structural_analysis.nested_model_triviality: An ellipse with free b/a subsumes the circle (b/a=1) as a special case. 'Never reversed' across V5-V9 is mathematically guaranteed for nested models — it proves nothing about physical reality.

why_it_matters: WIN-030 is a case study in circular reasoning through curve fitting. The dome model (1) takes great-circle distances from a sphere, (2) projects them onto a flat disc using an azimuthal equidistant projection anchored at πR_Earth, (3) notices the projection introduces systematic errors, (4) adds an elliptical correction to absorb those errors, then (5) claims the ellipse reveals the disc's physical shape. Every step depends on the globe. The 'discovery' of elliptical geometry is the dome model detecting its own spherical scaffolding.
suggested_text: The ellipse parameters (b/a ≈ 0.75, orientation ≈ 60°) are not derived from dome geometry — they are fitted via grid search in distance_analysis.py against distances the code itself labels "great circle" — distances defined by spherical trigonometry. The optimization target is the globe. Furthermore, the dome's core parameter file (inject_ai_layer.py) still defines a circular disc with radius 20,015 km — the ellipse appears only in visualization and analysis scripts, not in the model's foundational equations.
